event_label: Benny Goodman and His Swing Orchestra
speaker: Count Basie
caption: Count Basie recalls his first Carnegie Hall performance in 1938
text: Yes, I remember it very well. It was a great concert, and that was my first time at Carnegie Hall onstage. John had once taken me to hear Marion Anderson, which is something I will never forget.

event_label: Benny Goodman and His Swing Orchestra
speaker: Count Basie
caption: Recalling his first Carnegie Hall performance in 1938
text: Yeah, I know that was the first time I had ever been on the stage at Carnegie Hall.

event_label: Benny Goodman and His Swing Orchestra
speaker: Count Basie
caption: Basie recalls playing with Benny Goodman at Carnegie Hall
text: Oh, you've heard it. But that was a great time, the concert with Bennie, and the thrill of just being and working at Carnegie Hall -- it sort of gave you goose pimples, you know. (Inaudible) My fingers were kinda damp when I started to play.

event_label: Benny Goodman and His Swing Orchestra
speaker: Teddy Wilson
caption: Recalling the historic 1938 Benny Goodman concert
text: And then the next important part in the movie, I guess, they have us doing -- oh, after the band -- that's because the band leader in certain scenes where we're playing at the Palomar Ballroom in Los Angeles, I guess, and the success of the band, and all of a sudden, after flopping along the line, then the tremendous success. And then, of course, the movie ends with the Carnegie Hall Concert in 1938, with the tremendous triumph of the band, the first all jazz concert in Carnegie Hall.

event_label: Benny Goodman and His Swing Orchestra
speaker: Buck Clayton
caption: Buck Clayton recalls his first Carnegie Hall performance
text: Well that was my first time ever to play Carnegie Hall and it was the time that

event_label: From Spirituals to Swing
speaker: Helen Humes
caption: Helen Humes' nervous debut at From Spirituals to Swing
text: And I said, "oh my goodness I haven't even hummed since I've been home. I can't do nothing". He said, "yes you can Helen. Now you can sing." I said, "well it's been so long I don't know whether I can sing or not". He said, "well why don't you come on and try". I said, "because Carnegie Hall is too big to be trying to sing in". And so he said, "well, just come on, you can make it". And I said, "well, alright then, if you think I can make I'll come on and sing something". So I went on and Nellie Lutcher, bless her heart, she came and she said, "Helen I'll stay right here with you until you go on". She said, "don't be scared". And I said, "well you know every time I get nervous and I get hoarse and everything". She ran across the street to the drug store and she brought me more cough drops and cough syrups and stuff. And I said, "well if I ain't feeling better now, I won't". And I went on and it was sensational.

event_label: From Spirituals to Swing
speaker: Joe Turner
caption: Joe Turner recalls the first blues show at Carnegie Hall
text: During World War Number Two. And, uh, we were runnin' back and forth to New York an' all over the country. Gettin' back to New York, I was uh...forgot to tell you that the first blues show that ever was at Carnegie Hall, John Hammond gave it, an' I was on the show. Had Big Bill from Chicago, a host of other blues singers... Big Bill had on some tight shoes were hurtin' his feet. He would stand up on the stage playin' and cryin'.

event_label: Café Society Concert
speaker: Bill Coleman
caption: 1943 benefit concert with Hazel Scott and Frank Orchard
text: World war number two had been going on for nearly two years in America. The trio was beginning to become popular, and we co-starred with Hazel Scott after her return from Hollywood for a benefit to buy watches for the Soviet Army, which had taken place at Carnegie Hall the 11th of April, 1943. The trio did a few numbers and for the finale, we were joined by a trombonist named Frank Orchard, and the trumpetist Jack Butler.

event_label: Woody Herman and His Orchestra
speaker: Woody Herman
caption: Woody Herman's first jazz concert at Carnegie Hall in 1946
text: And -- but basically, it was his music, as it should have been. And we learned to play the music, and presented it at Carnegie Hall in 1946, at our first jazz concert. And the critique was quite broad, some of it with great gusto and enjoyment and some of it couldn't understand what it was about and so consequently I would say it would remind me of some of the things that still happen.

event_label: Midnight Performance: Pittsburgh Courier Poll Winners Jazz Concert
speaker: Arnett Cobb
caption: 1947 Pittsburgh Courier Poll Winners Jazz Concert
text: Yeah. I tell you what -- I played a Pittsburgh Courier thing at Carnegie Hall with him at midnight and we received awards, and I was in the running of the saxophone players for the Pittsburgh Courier. And, of course, we had to stay up until 8:00 o'clock that morning going into Columbus, and this is when I made up my mind I was going to put my notice in.

event_label: Carnegie Pop Concert
speaker: Sam Wooding
caption: Sam Wooding recalls Carnegie Hall performance with Kenneth Spencer
text: And then that's the choir that played with Kenny Spencer and we played the "Ballad of America", you know, that Paul Robeson used to do. Kenneth Spencer did it and then we had the evening in Carnegie Hall. And we had quite a job. That's the one when I walked into Rae. And her mother brought Rae to me at that time, you see.

event_label: Bunk Johnson's Band
speaker: Garvin Bushell
caption: Garvin Bushell recalls recording with Bunk Johnson in heavy snow
text: Carnegie Hall. We recorded it in Carnegie Hall and we went into Carnegie Hall at 12:00 o'clock -- oh, (Well upon) Wilman Braud was the base player, not Pop Foster, Broe. William Braud, that's the name -- he's from New Orleans. And we went in at 12:00 o'clock and it just began to snow. When we came out at 5:00 that evening the busses were straddling the roads. There was snow, I think, three feet deep. That's how hard it had snowed in New York while we were recording. I remember Bunk saying -- he'd never seen snow, you know. He had never seen snow.

event_label: Billie Holiday, Singer
speaker: John Levy
caption: John Levy recalls Billie Holiday's 1948 Carnegie Hall debut
text: we got a job to go to 52nd Street and open in the Onyx Club. And in those days they gave you a long term deal, so the deal was for six months. And Jimmy Jones, I’m sort of ahead of myself, because actually Jimmy Jones joined the group — Raymond Walters was the first pianist with us, and he worked with us maybe in Chicago for maybe about two or three months. And then Jimmy Jones and I had been friends, I knew him as a player and I recommended that he come on with us. And that’s how the real Stuff Smith Trio got together. And then we came to New York and opened in the Onyx Club in August of 1944. And then I met Joe Glaser for the first time — this agent and everything. And as I said I developed an excellent relationship with him, and learned a lot about the inside of the business and how it all worked, which helped me later on when I finally went into that end of the business. We worked there at the Onyx Club — I guess we stayed there about a year. And I think I left, I was the first one to leave the trio. I went with Don Byas for a while, and then did a lot of recordings with Ink Spots and you name it, different jazz groups, and then Errol Garner. I made a series of records with Errol, just four sides actually, for Savoy Records with Errol Garner. And from that, one thing to the other until we get to 1948. In 1948 I got a call from Bobby Tucker to join him to work with Billie Holiday. And Billie Holiday had been in Lexington, Kentucky for a rehab for the dope. And she was coming out, and she was going to do a Carnegie Hall concert. And this was 1948. So we got together, and Denzel Best was the drummer. And we rehearsed out in New Jersey at Bobby Tucker’s house, where Billie Holiday was staying. And rehearsed for that. And I can’t remember the exact date in 1948 that we played Carnegie Hall, but we played it. It was my first time in Carnegie Hall. It was the first time that Carnegie Hall had ever had just a trio playing on stage, you know, so I mean the union made a ruling for the future that you’d have to have a minimum number of twelve musicians to play Carnegie Hall.

event_label: Billie Holiday, Singer
speaker: John Levy
caption: Billie Holiday's 1948 comeback concert at Carnegie Hall
text: My name is John Mitchell. I’m a staff writer for the Los Angeles Times. Today is December 10th, 2006. We’re in the home of John Levy and his wife Devra Hall at 1827 Coolidge Avenue, Altadena, California. We’re here as part of the Smithsonian’s Jazz Oral History Project to record John Levy’s story. A former bass player, John has been managing the careers of music artists since 1951. In his musical prime he played the bass when jazz was at its heyday on New York’s 52nd Street. Duke Ellington wanted him in his orchestra. Billie Holiday hired him for her comeback concert at Carnegie Hall in 1948. Then John put away his upright to sit behind a desk and manage the careers of artists. He had a rich stable of clients: George Shearing, Dakota Staton, Betty Carter, Lou Donaldson, Herbie Mann, Les McCann, Ernie Andrews, Randy Crawford, Brook Benton, Ahmad Jamal, Freddie Hubbard, Cannonball Adderley, Wes Montgomery, Joe Williams, Shirley Horn, Ramsey Lewis, Roberta Flack, Donny Hathaway, Eddie Harris, Johnny Hartman, Stanley Turrentine, Diane Reeves, Sarah Vaughan, Nancy Wilson, and the list goes on. So much history. So many lives touched. We want to start at the beginning. So John, why don’t you start by giving us your full name and spelling it for us.

event_label: Midnight Performance: Pittsburgh Courier Poll Winners Jazz Concert
speaker: Erskine Hawkins
caption: Hawkins recalls performing at Carnegie Hall during big band era
text: Well, I played most of the big spots. I did a concert in Carnegie Hall. That was during the big band years which I, during the time when they had a paper called Pittsburgh Courier out at that time. That was one of the highlights. When I first went into, I was looking forward, I played the Apollo and all them now cause I loved that. But, I was looking forward to going downtown and playing the Paramount, the Strand and all that. And

event_label: Midnight Performance: Pittsburgh Courier Charity Fund's 7th Annual Concert
speaker: Slide Hampton
caption: Hampton family band's 1952 Carnegie Hall debut
text: In 1952, music took you to Carnegie Hall with the Hampton band. At that point, you had been performing trombone professionally, touring. I think of it as barnstorming. I’d like you to respond to that. But I want you to tell the record, how the Hampton – not the Lionel Hampton band, but the Hampton family band – got to Carnegie Hall in 1952.

event_label: Midnight Performance: Pittsburgh Courier Charity Fund's 7th Annual Concert
speaker: Slide Hampton
caption: Winning a contest to open for Lionel Hampton at Carnegie Hall
text: Besides practicing, there was a contest that Lionel Hampton’s band was set up to do this. They were set up to do this, and part of the attraction to wanting to win this competition was that you were going to get to play – open the show for Lionel Hampton’s band. We got votes and got people to vote for us. We got enough votes to win this contest. We won it, and we played Carnegie Hall, opening the show for Lionel Hampton’s band.

event_label: Midnight Performance: Pittsburgh Courier Charity Fund's 7th Annual Concert
speaker: Slide Hampton
caption: Slide Hampton's 1952 Carnegie Hall debut
text: When you played at Carnegie Hall in ’52, could you visualize yourself ultimately being in the Lionel Hampton band?

event_label: Jazz Concert featuring Duke Ellington, Billie Holiday, Charlie Parker and Others
speaker: Roy Haynes
caption: 1952 Carnegie Hall gig with Duke Ellington and Charlie Parker
text: But I didn’t know them. You know what? Duke’s band – when I was much younger, it was the hardest thing to get close to. Even Sonny Greer. I didn’t get – Sonny Greer, late in life, we were very close. I used to go to his gigs. He lived near Michael’s. He would come over there sometimes to see me and see these pretty drums. He was always – you ever see the set of drums that Sonny Greer would have? Jo Jones used to call them the Empire State Building. He had drums on wheels. He had tympanis. He had his initial on every drum, S. G. I had a picture of it at home when I was a teenager. He had the chimes, for something. When – I guess it was 1952, when Louie Bellson was leaving the band. He had married Pearl Bailey. He was going to Europe, I think, for a honeymoon. So maybe a few nights before that we were playing at Carnegie Hall. I was with Charlie Parker, and Duke’s band was there. Joe Morgan was doing p.r. for Duke. He kept instigating to get me in the band. I was living at the Hotel President on West 48th Street, right in front of Mama Leone’s, at the time. Duke called me up there, and he talked a lot. He didn’t really say – ask me to join the band, but he talked a lot then. But that’s what he wanted. I would always meet him at a restaurant up in Harlem called Ma Frazier’s. He always would mention the fact

event_label: Jazz Concert featuring Count Basie and His Orchestra, Charlie Parker, and Sarah Vaughan
speaker: Frank Foster
caption: 1954 Pittsburgh Courier Jazz Poll All-Stars concert
text: Ballroom, the track. This program at Carnegie Hall was one of those gala all stars. Actually the winners of the Pittsburgh Courier music poll -- I don’t know if they called it the jazz poll. It probably was a jazz poll -- but such stars as Lucky Millinder, Bill Eckstine, Count Basie, Sarah Vaughan, Duke Ellington.

event_label: Jazz Concert featuring Count Basie and His Orchestra, Charlie Parker, and Sarah Vaughan
speaker: Frank Foster
caption: Frank Foster recalls limited set time at 1954 Jazz Concert
text: at Central State. I’ll call it Central State, although it wasn’t called that at the time, just to simplify matters. My mother was teaching in the English and Speech Department. She took off to -­ actually I suppose to chaperone me. We stayed at my cousin’s house in Harlem. The rest of the band stayed at this -- wherever the hotel was. I don’t even know where the hotel was. We played opposite the house band at the -- not the Apollo, at the Savoy Ballroom, the now defunct Savoy Ballroom, which was a great musical landmark in Harlem up until it was destroyed in I don’t know when. In the ’50s, I guess [1958]. It’s replaced by a housing development or something [Delano Village]. I don’t who the house band was at the time, but it was just a thrill to me to be appearing at New York’s Savoy Ballroom. I was in heaven. And on stage at Carnegie Hall there were so many artists appearing that our time was very limited. I suppose we had about 20 minutes originally. Because Lucky Millinder got long winded on stage and went over his time limit, they cut us down to about ten minutes. We performed two songs. But it was a high point, if I may, in my young career.

event_label: Jazz Concert featuring Count Basie and His Orchestra, Charlie Parker, and Sarah Vaughan
speaker: Percy Heath
caption: Percy Heath recalls performing with Charlie Parker at Carnegie Hall
text: No, he wasn’t kicked out. He just didn’t wanna do all that rehearsing and playing drum parts. Kenny did not want to play that kind of music. He was an innovator on his own as far as his instrument was concerned. He started [a] certain concept of punctuating. If you hear that thing that we did before the Modern Jazz Quartet, we were on a thing at Carnegie Hall with Charlie Parker, there’s a record out of that with Kenny and myself and John Lewis, and you can hear Kenny in the back, “Go Bird! That kind of bebop.” Kenny Clarke, he was it. Max was [had] a certain recognition being with Charlie Parker for his technique and what not, but Kenny Clarke swung. Miles used to say, “Max couldn’t swing if you hung him by his neck,” but he was correct. And technically, impossible to do what he was doing. Arthur Taylor used to sit around all day with Max Roach records trying to play what Max Roach was playing. But as far as swing, Jo Jones, “Philly Joe,” and “Papa Jo,” they call him, they had that swing. Shadow Wilson, he could swing. Denzel Best was a quiet but swing. You can play, a lot of people can play, but that feeling, that’s the difference between jazz and other music all over the planet. “It don’t mean a thing,” like the Maestro said, “if it ain’t got that swing.” Well, that swing is the difference like Buhaina. Art Blakey will swing you into bad health, but you had to hold him back ‘cause the tempos would go up. And the marriage between bass players and drummers in order to keep that swing, like Kenny Clarke, you got to play behind him a little bit, behind the beat. Art Blakey gotta lay back a little bit. Connie Kay was so right on it that you feel like you gotta pull him a little bit. That difference of where on the pulse that the bass player and the drums marry, that’s the difference in a formation swinging and not giving you that feeling. You say, “Oh you gotta pat your foot to that.” So, different drummers have different points of where the beat is in his head, and that’s what happened with the Quartet. Everybody felt where one was. You had to know where one is, and Lester Young played so fluent, but when Prez said, “BOOP!” it was right on it! But he’d be laying back a little relaxed and what not, but when Lester said, [sings melody], that beat said, “Here it is!” There’s no doubt that was one. This concept of jazz, I really wanna emphasize that even though the Quartet had this polyphonic concept of

event_label: Billie Holiday: Lady Sings the Blues
speaker: Kenny Burrell
caption: Burrell recalls playing live at Carnegie Hall with Billie Holiday
text: Well, that’s another situation that stared in Detroit, again, by that time when she used to come, I would say that this is in the early 50’s. I had a pretty popular band, you know, I was working around town a lot and she used to come and work at this place called the Rouge Lounge, the big club I told you about. So I had worked there with my group and the owner, I can’t think of his name at the moment, but the point is that he would call me to back up Billie Holiday. I got to play with her and get to know her, and rehearse with her and she likes my music, she likes me and there was never any problem. She was always very frank, she didn’t hold back. If she wanted something she’d tell you and she wouldn’t, you know, suffer through anything, she’d just say, “No, no, lets do it this way.” But in a beautiful way she was a lady day, you know, a lady. Unfortunately, you know, we all know the story of how she was abused and misused and taken advantage of, but she was such a, to me, a gentle tortured soul. So I met her then. Then later on when I moved to New York in the, ’56, you know, after I had been with Peterson, I had got a call from various contractors and different people and I think for some reason Ralph Burns was associated with some of those records I made with Billie Holiday. Oh no, no, the first one I think was live At Carnegie Hall.

event_label: Billie Holiday: Lady Sings the Blues
speaker: Kenny Burrell
caption: Burrell recalls performing with Billie Holiday at Carnegie Hall
text: Right, and that was, I don’t know how that came about. I know Tony Scott was on it, I think Chico Hamilton, I’m not sure if Chico was on there, but anyway, it was a date where they used some young guys and not so young guys. Anyway, I was fortunate enough to be on it. That was kind of a historic date, you know, because it was Carnegie Hall and so forth. But then I also remember doing some studio dates with her, and these were some small dates. I think it was Harry Edison and either Ben Webster of Sam “The Man” Taylor, you know, the rhythm section. I just enjoyed playing for her as we say among the musicians that she never sang one note she didn't mean. And as we were talking earlier about how you’re connected to your deep self, well, she certainly was connected and that’s why she got to so many people in terms of connecting to audiences and communicating. She was a wonderful, wonderful singer who influenced so many singers, male and female. Sinatra, and all kinds of people she influenced and musicians. In the sense that you express your feelings in your music and she was not ashamed to do that and I think probably the obvious example of that is Strange Fruit. The courage and

event_label: Billie Holiday: Lady Sings the Blues
speaker: Chico Hamilton
caption: Hamilton recalls performing with Billie Holiday at Carnegie Hall
text: Oh, yeah, we’re still friends. I don’t see her, but she was cool. She had her good days and bad days like all them singers, you know. So I’d advise any young player to go play for a singer, lady singer. Don’t pick up her bag to help out. Don’t pick up because man, that’s what you’ll be doing, which is cool. And man I played for, you know, Lady. Lady and I, That Lady Sings the Blues at Carnegie Hall. But the heaviest time I ever laid man was when we played – Hamp, Wardell, and myself, and Curtis Counce, at the 331 Club in LA. Is that the name of it, 331?

event_label: Tony Bennett, Singer
speaker: Candido Camero
caption: Candido recalls recording with Tony Bennett at Carnegie Hall
text: Well, I went to work with Tony Bennett at the time. I went to work with Tony Bennett and also we toured the entire United States. Then we went to South America, Canada and Venezuela and I recorded with him a number of times too - a huge concert at Carnegie Hall that was recorded. And now in the book on his life which is entitled, Better Life, he mentions my name several times and speaks highly of me and aside from that, he has also included some pictures of me in the book where I appear working with him. That was the first recording I did with him that is called The Beat of My Heart by Tony Bennett.

event_label: Newport Jazz Festival: Weather Report, Roy Haynes Hip Ensemble, Tony Williams's Lifetime, and the Archie Schepp Quintet
speaker: Roy Haynes
caption: 1972 Carnegie Hall concert with Lifetime, Weather Report, and Shepp
text: Yeah. Some of it was headed that direction. That’s when I started getting the young audiences. Then I started – I had – I can’t even think of the guy’s name. He’s in California now. I had some good musicians. Some of them worked with Miles. I had some good cats there. I had some good times, too. When I had the name – we played once at Carnegie Hall with Tony Williams, with Lifetime, Archie Shepp, Weather Report, my group. We burnin’. We had a standing ovation. We went to Europe, Japan. I had a nice little run with it. But later on, after I stopped the Hip Ensemble, then they start calling my groups Hip Ensemble when they weren’t Hip Ensemble. The Hip Ensemble was that one period of time when I was using the title. After that, then they wanted it. At first it seemed like they didn’t want to accept the name. We would close with the black national anthem. I have a drum solo and go into that. Close, close out the show.

event_label: Hubert Laws Afro-Classic Ensemble/ Randy Weston Quintet
speaker: Hubert Laws
caption: Hubert Laws recalls the Jan 12, 1973 Carnegie Hall concert
text: You talked about Eric Gale yesterday, bemoaning the fact that so many of the CTI artists are not with us. Another one of those killer albums, Eric Gale’s Forecast. I remember it well. You’re listed flute and piccolo on this one. Killing Me Softly, Dindi – which, that was big. I know when Wayne first put that on, I think, Super Nova. I think that might have been it. It might have been Super Nova or one of those ones. But anytime – again, Creed Taylor putting the rhythm section out there. He was going to make sure that everybody go their due. Carnegie Hall Concert, January 12th, 1973. You got – this is pretty interesting, because of the – I remember this cover. It’s got the multiple pictures of you all across.

event_label: Hubert Laws Afro-Classic Ensemble/ Randy Weston Quintet
speaker: Hubert Laws
caption: Hubert Laws recalls 1973 Afro-Classic Ensemble concert with Billy Taylor
text: I remember Billy Taylor was so excited about that record, because I remember we did a Carnegie Hall concert. I had Debra and Eloise and Ronnie. They all played on it. Had a big band, because CBS sponsored the – all these instruments. I had trumpets and trombones, because that’s what’s on that record. They supported strings. Back then, that’s when the record companies got behind supporting you with additional instruments and all that. I never will forget, boy, people in the aisle just rocking with that song. Billy Taylor came to the dressing room and said, “Man, that was a tremendous piece.” He complimented me on that. That meant a lot to me, because, you know, Billy Taylor’s a bebopper.

event_label: Miles Davis and His Group
speaker: Dave Liebman
caption: Liebman recalls a tense gig with Miles Davis at Carnegie Hall
text: I have no idea. The idea was to get rid of me, obviously. Finally Mtume found a way to do it. Azar comes out and cannot – doesn’t know what to do. Miles goes like this – give him my horn, because he didn’t have a pickup. I said, no way. I was pissed off. My mother and father were there. I think Joe Allard was there. I’m at Carnegie Hall with Miles Davis, and you’re going to bring out a ringer for me? I said, I ain’t taking this shit. I was pissed off. I was really pissed off. The nerve of him. Plus he came an hour­ and-a-half late from 20 blocks away. How are you an hour-and-a-half late when you are from 78th Street to 58th Street? Can I get this right? He comes in. It’s everybody, overtime at Carnegie Hall. George [?]. Everybody’s backstage flipping out. It’s Carnegie Hall. It’s New York City. Guy comes in late, high as a kite. Not one word. Out from the car right to the stage, dressed in complete black. That was not a great gig. But I – I had to write notes for it, whenever they re-released it.

event_label: New York Jazz Repertory Company: Salute to Thelonious Monk
speaker: Barry Harris
caption: Barry Harris recalls Thelonious Monk's surprise performance at Carnegie Hall
text: Probably had something to do with the musicians who were on the concert. Something was wrong, I ain’t going to mention any names about that concert, but something was wrong. So he came, he laid in my bed for ten years, and um I had to sleep in another bed. He stayed there – one day we did go to the piano, he made me play My Ideal. He played My Ideal over and over and over and over and over. It’d be fifty choruses apiece. Unfortunately it wasn’t recorded, it should have been recorded. Then – then there was one concert, it was a Monk concert going on at Carnegie Hall, with strings and stuff, and I was rehearsing with the band, and with the strings and stuff. And the day of the concert, I happened to ask Monk, I said, “Monk, why don’t you get up and play this concert?” See the funny thing about Monk – generally he’s gonna let you know if he’s not gonna do something. But he didn’t say anything. So when he didn’t say anything, I said, “huh, that’s funny.” I went downstairs, I had to leave for rehearsal, and I told the people downstairs, I said, “ask him if he wants to play for this concert, ‘cause he didn’t say no.” So I go to rehearsal, and the concert is on, we get there, Carnegie Hall, tuning up and stuff, about ready to hit it, about eight o’clock – hit time. Getting ready to hit – go out on the stage and in the door walked Monk. He got up out of bed and came and played the concert. So he played that whole concert, I don’t think I can say I got paid for free. So, then after Monk – what else. I gave some more – right after Monk died, that’s when I went to jazz culture theater. ‘Cause one of the first concerts I gave is – was dedicated to Monk, 1982. There’s one restaurant right now on 45th street between 8th and 9th avenue named Kodama, they have a picture of this concert that was dedicated to Monk. The poster, a big beautiful poster, they have it hanging up in that restaurant. It’s been hanging there for twenty years. Over – no it’s been hanging there longer than that. It’s been hanging in that restaurant – ‘cause I think that restaurant opened up about 1982 or something. So it’s been hanging in that restaurant – no, that’s 24, 26 years. No, it’s 28 years. It’s still there. And let’s see, what other things are interest – I travel. I travel the world, I teach all over the world. Every place – every place I teach is like home. They cry

event_label: New York Jazz Repertory Company: A Musical Salute to Bud Freeman/ The Music of Fletcher Henderson
speaker: Bud Freeman
caption: Bud Freeman recalls 50th anniversary concert with Jess Stacy
text: Now, most interesting thing, I don't want to dismiss talking about Jess, but as you know, I did not see, over a period of years, I did not see too much of him and didn't have a chance to work with him. But this is the thing that happened, George Wein gave me a 50th anniversary concert, I think you were there, or I think Stanley was there, at Carnegie Hall about four years ago, and Jess came from the Coast and played on the show.

event_label: An Evening with Jon Lucien, featuring Special Guests Stanley Turrentine and George Benson
speaker: Hubert Laws
caption: Hubert Laws recalls performing with George Benson at Carnegie Hall
text: I always had respect for the instrument – great playing guitarists. But particularly George Benson. He was one who I felt did not get as much recognition as a guitar player as he should have, because he was – he still is. I saw him in Moscow not long ago, I think a year ago when we played Moscow, at the airport. I said, “Man, let me tell you. People don’t know, but you are a great guitar – you’re my favorite guitar player.” One of my favorites, anyway. George never read music too much, either. That’s what’s so amazing. This guy had such great ears. I wondered how he can go in these sessions and play, because he’s got such good memory and such good facility. He played on my first record, I think. Yeah, my very first record, Crying Song, for CTI. Creed put all these people together. Have this special sound. That’s how I met George. Then I ended up playing on live concerts with him at Carnegie Hall, and we did that record. You say White Rabbit. I have to remember that record by listening to it again. It’s been a while. He’s just – as you well know, he had this great singing voice that Creed never really tapped into. I remember the time when we were on tour, the first tour in Japan. We arrived at the airport. They had people out. We were like rock stars. These people had these huge banners out, “Welcome CTI All-Stars,” young girls jumping up and down. They treated us like rock stars. George Benson – on that tour, George Benson, Bob James, Ron Carter, Jack Dejohnette – I think in some cases it was Billy Cobham – Hank Crawford, Johnny Hammond. Esther Phillips was there too, the first tour. I remember it, because – Hank Crawford and Fred would get into it on the bus. They had this nice bus, all equipped with everything we needed. Like I told you, we felt like rock stars. Freddie and Hank would get into these disputes about music. They both got high, because Hank liked that cognac. He was just drinking cognac like mad. So he started – he’d get on Freddie’s case, and they’d start talking about each other’s musicality. Freddie would call him a little – he’d call Hank a tweety bird. Tweety bird sound. “Man, what kind of sound is that you’ve got on that thing?” I don’t know what Hank’s rebuttal was, but it wasn’t strong, but Freddie was just too tremendous in terms of his playing. The reason why I brought this up is because that’s one of the times that George Benson displayed his wonderful singing quality. He was able to imitate. He imitated Esther Phillips better than anybody, right there on the bus. He went ih-ih-ih-ih-ih-ih, that nanny- goat vibrato and all that. He had it covered. But nobody ever used his singing until he signed with Warner Bros. and did Masquerade. Then nobody looked – he didn’t look back after that. I wonder how Creed felt about that, because Creed – I don’t think he ever produced – I don’t know if he ever produced any singers. But I don’t know why he didn’t

event_label: New York Jazz Repertory Company: The Music of Bix Beiderbecke
speaker: Spiegle Willcox
caption: Recreating Bix Beiderbecke's 1975 Carnegie Hall concert
text: Well it’s just because I’ve lived so long and, that is interesting, because I played, I had a band of my own around Cornell, Colgate, Syracuse for years, like a ten, twelve piece band, and very popular. And then as rock & roll kept coming in later on you know and I got older, so I did less playing. I didn’t think rock & roll was going to last but, and you know sometimes people ask me, “Do you have a favorite rock group?” And I say, “Yeah, Mount Rushmore.” But anyway, that’s what happened. Then finally in 1975, I’ll tell this as fast as I can, in 1975 I was invited to go down to New York City. Why? To re-create a concert in Carnegie Hall of Bix Beiderbecke’s short life with the Goldkette band. And we © Fillius Jazz Archive -5-

event_label: Superstars II
speaker: Harold Danko
caption: First Carnegie Hall performance with Chet Baker
text: Yeah sometimes, and some tunes just to remind myself too, to get back to see where I am. And I think keeping the melody close is very important. And also it’s a reference point and if you can develop that, you know Lee Konitz had this kind of one to ten thing where if you’re playing the melody that’s number one and if you’re playing something that’s totally unlike the melody, you know if you’re playing bebop maybe that’s number five or number six. If you’re playing really abstractly that’s number ten, but you’re still playing the tune. So Lee was very specific about saying yeah you should be able to play jazz at number two and number three, that that still sounds like jazz. Because a lot of people just have their prepared lick repertoire or whatever, and so they play the melody and then they go into whatever. And he said they’re jumping maybe from number one to number six, and it’s like so how do you — and so there’s a thing that I’m on with Lee it’s called “Portrait of a Saxophonist as an Artist” it’s a video that we did in Canada. And Lee is trying to get this flute player to play something close to the melody. Because if you play these other things, and when he played close to the melody it didn’t sound like jazz you know, it just sounded suddenly stiff. And so that’s the point of that, and then funny in that video the next thing goes to Lee playing very abstractly on “Stella by Starlight” or something like that, just the most — but you know Lee had me singing Lester Young solos and Louis Armstrong. I basically did not study with Lee but in the course of rehearsing I got a lot of the things that he taught people. And he was very insistent on, like Lee once said, once told me I don’t know I was playing “All the Things You Are” or something, and he accused me of using too much technique. I said man you want chops, there’s a hundred guys in New York — I didn’t say this but I’m thinking like you want chops? I’m not one of the chop guys. There’s guys that will play rings around me. You want chops? So Lee said, “Well it should basically be able to play a good solo with one finger.” And he’d be like [plays piano] whatever it was you know. So okay yeah. So one of the things I have to remind myself of, it came really in handy because the first time I played Carnegie Hall with Chet Baker, the first time in Carnegie Hall, I remember we were playing “Tidal Breeze” my tune. And I looked down and my knee was going up and down, I had this nervous thing. And I thought of that thing that Lee — I said I know my tune I can just kind of play the melody, I can play really close and just let the rhythm section groove. And then my knee calmed down and I was able to — but it was like one of those little nervous things, like the tic that could define your career. So that thing of being able to play it and have it sound like it’s jazz but very simple. It’s really hard, and it’s like I want to show what I can do sometimes, even at my age. But sometimes it’s just © Fillius Jazz Archive -14-

event_label: Newport Jazz Festival: The Ellington Saga, Part II
speaker: Pug Horton
caption: Pug Horton recalls performing Ivy Anderson's music with Bob
text: Yeah. And we laugh a little bit about that you know. But we know where we’re going and what we’re going to do, and we like to think of music as being theatre. When Bob was director of the Smithsonian, we put on a theatrical performance. It began with me singing a cappella “Back Water Blues,” and then went into “Mournful Serenade.” On a darkened stage, Bob would walk out onto a darkened stage and talk about what is jazz, and then would build. And we would do an hour and a half of the whole history of jazz, starting with Bessie Smith and Eva Taylor and we’d start the second half with Duke Ellington and I would do the Ivy Anderson. That’s what I did with Bob. Our first gig together was at Carnegie Hall and he asked me to do the music of Ivy Anderson. I said she wasn’t really somebody that I had listened to a great deal, and I thought thank you very much, it was nice of him to ask but I wouldn’t bother. But I digress on that. I did tell him that I didn’t want to do it but he persuaded me that I should. But in any case we © Fillius Jazz Archive -10-

event_label: Paul Whiteman Rediscovered
speaker: Al Gallodoro
caption: Recreating Bix Beiderbecke's 1975 Carnegie Hall concert
text: Well it’s just because I’ve lived so long and, that is interesting, because I played, I had a band of my own around Cornell, Colgate, Syracuse for years, like a ten, twelve piece band, and very popular. And then as rock & roll kept coming in later on you know and I got older, so I did less playing. I didn’t think rock & roll was going to last but, and you know sometimes people ask me, “Do you have a favorite rock group?” And I say, “Yeah, Mount Rushmore.” But anyway, that’s what happened. Then finally in 1975, I’ll tell this as fast as I can, in 1975 I was invited to go down to New York City. Why? To re-create a concert in Carnegie Hall of Bix Beiderbecke’s short life with the Goldkette band. And we © Fillius Jazz Archive -5-

event_label: Paul Whiteman Rediscovered
speaker: Al Gallodoro
caption: Al Gallodoro recalls performing at Paul Whiteman Rediscovered in 1976
text: I did the Paul Whiteman Rediscovered. Around 1977. We opened at the Academy of Music in Philadelphia, standing room, two days later we went to Carnegie Hall, standing room, we were supposed to go on the road and nothing happened. © Fillius Jazz Archive -8-

event_label: Woody Herman and His Thundering Herd: 40th Anniversary Concert
speaker: Woody Herman
caption: Woody Herman's 40th Anniversary Concert at Carnegie Hall
text: Uh-huh. Your 40th anniversary had a lot of attention. It was a true milestone, and your concert at Carnegie Hall brought a lot of your alumni together.

event_label: Woody Herman and His Thundering Herd: 40th Anniversary Concert
speaker: Jake Hanna
caption: Woody Herman skipping Woodchopper's Ball at 40th anniversary
text: Oh, well Woody Herman, he played the “Woodchopper’s Ball” every night. Yeah. We played Carnegie Hall one night and he says, “You notice something tonight at the fortieth anniversary?” I said, “No.” “We got away without playing the ‘Woodchopper’s Ball.’” First time he’s ever able to do it in about 30, 40 years.

event_label: Benny Goodman 40th Anniversary Concert at Carnegie Hall
speaker: Jack Sheldon
caption: Jack Sheldon recalls performing at Benny Goodman's 40th Anniversary Concert
text: Yeah. I’ve been there. What was the last time, oh yeah Carnegie Hall with Rosemary Clooney, yeah that was a thrill, God I loved her. And I’ve done Carnegie Hall with Benny a few times, we did the 40 year anniversary, and then I did a few concerts with Benny back then, and I got to go back with Rosemary Clooney, we did a tribute to Bing Crosby. I love here, she’s a great artist.

event_label: Newport Jazz Festival: Three Pianos and Two Guitars/ Irakere
speaker: Paquito D'Rivera
caption: 1978 Carnegie Hall performance with Bebo Valdés visiting
text: We played Carnegie Hall in 1978, and it was a coincidence that Bebo Valdés was visiting New York when that was happening. So, he got the chance to see his son after almost 20 years without seeing him.

event_label: Newport Jazz Festival: Three Pianos and Two Guitars/ Irakere
speaker: Paquito D'Rivera
caption: Irakere's 1978 Carnegie Hall debut
text: Let’s go back to 78, when Irakere played at Carnegie Hall; what was the response of that audience?

event_label: Newport Jazz Festival: Tribute to Lionel Hampton
speaker: Arnett Cobb
caption: Arnett Cobb recalls playing with Lionel Hampton at Carnegie Hall on July 1, 1978
text: Yes, going back with Lionel, (inaudible). We're leaving on the 29th of April. We (rehearse) (inaudible) on the 30th of June -- I mean -- 29th of April -- 29th of June and rehearse on the 30th of June and the first of July. We play a concert at Carnegie Hall on the first, that night, of July. We go to Saratoga on the 2nd, and we're off on the third and leave on the 4th, get into Nice on the 5th. We have --

event_label: Tribute to Charles Mingus: Mingus Music
speaker: Onaje Allan Gumbs
caption: Onaje Allan Gumbs recalls the 1979 Mingus tribute concert
text: You can hear an orchestra doing this. Carter brought this tune in called “Rise from Atlantis.” It had the same effect on me. I could hear the horns. I could hear the strings. I could hear the percussion. I mean it was an awesome tune. So Carter, I says, this was well worth the wait, you know, for your not contributing any music in this band. And we had a couple of anecdotes with Woody that were priceless. Because we were asked to do a tribute to Mingus when Mingus passed away. At Carnegie Hall. And of course the traditional thing, when you pay homage to a fallen soldier, you play his music. Well at the rehearsal Woody says, “I ain’t playing no Mingus music.” We say “Woody” — you know my brain is going man— He says “if they don’t want to hear my music that’s too bad. I’m playing my music.” That’s not the point, we’re supposed to — so what happened, I had to think quick. Because I’m part of this band, so whatever he does, I’m part of it. So I said well I’ve got to do something. I’d written this tune, which served both purposes. It helped keep him doing his band music, yet still pay homage to Mingus. I’d written this tune called “You Never Said You Had Go.” And he presented it in that context, as to why I’d written this music, just for Mingus. I remember — my wife doesn’t remember this but I talked about it and she was at the concert. She said she started crying for the sentiment of the tune. Because Mingus died kind of suddenly. So that’s why I said “You Never Said you Had to Go.” So I was like wheew, man, to get out of that one. Because at the rehearsal I was like oh sh— I can’t say that word right. And we got through it, we got through that. But you know more recently I’ve really been trying to just enjoy all the music, and enjoy everything that’s out there.

event_label: Kool Jazz Festival: An Evening with Joe Williams and Friends
speaker: Norman Simmons
caption: Norman Simmons recalls joining Joe Williams' band
text: Ellis. Joe had been using Ellis in New York. And Ellis had gotten his permanent gig, the guy opened the Carnegie Tavern right behind Carnegie Hall and gave Ellis this gig as a house — that was his gig. So that’s how I happened to get the call and go in there. And so since Paul couldn’t travel, and I’d known John from Chicago too originally. And he’s always been in my corner. And so he said, “get Norman to do it.” So actually when Joe called me he didn’t go through any official hiring. He called me and said, “John Levy’s going to send you the itinerary, and you pick out what you want to do.” That was his way of saying the job was mine.

event_label: 50th Anniversary of Benny Goodman's 1938 Carnegie Hall Concert
speaker: Bob Wilber
caption: Recreating Benny Goodman's 1938 Carnegie Hall concert
text: Yeah. oh yeah. I mean I’ve had that problem. But one of the reasons I’ve done these tributes and everything, I just want to somehow bring people’s attention to this music. You’ve got to know about this music and hear it. Another reason was economic because when I started playing with a big band, using big bands, the only way you could get a promoter to book you would be to play a tribute to Benny Goodman or a tribute to Duke Ellington. Just to have Bob Wilber and his band, it’s not going to draw people. So I did a lot of these tributes. I suppose the most famous one I did was the re-creation of Benny Goodman’s Carnegie Hall concert, the 1938 concert. We did it 50 years to the night from the original concert at Carnegie Hall. It was note for note the same songs. We changed the order a little bit because we felt it would make it better in terms of pace. We had the same sound set up, one hanging mic and one mic on stage for Pug [Wilber] to use for vocals. It was totally sold out, a big success. And that was one of the high points. Another high point was when we presented the world premier of “The Queen Suite” at the Royal Festival Hall in Great Britain. You know the story of “The Queen Suite” was that Duke met the Queen in 1957 when he was touring England. And Duke was a sucker for anything to do with royalty and all, and he just loved it, it was marvelous. So he goes home back to the United States and he’s touring around and he writes this work and he calls it “The Queen Suite” and he goes back to New York, he goes into the studio and records it, and has one LP made and sent to the Queen. And he says in his letter, “Henceforth this is only for your ears, Your Majesty.” And he never played the work in its entirety again. He would sometimes play one of the pieces, “A Single Petal of the Rose,” as a piano solo. So after he died, Norman Granz got ahold of the tape and issued it on Pablo. So we had this idea of doing “The Queen Suite” and I was able to put the earphones on and transcribe it from the recording.

event_label: JVC Jazz Festival: For the Love of Louis
speaker: Randy Sandke
caption: Randy Sandke recalls a difficult small group performance at Carnegie Hall
text: Yeah. I mean I don’t know that that’s so much a generational thing because I think it’s always been like that. There’ve been a few players that can just make it sing and take wings and whatever, and a lot of people for whatever reason, it’s just less than that. And there are some players these days that I think are quite lyrical, and even younger players, and then guys who are not. One thing, going back to another thing about playing, one thing that sort of interests me, I mean I guess is that Louis Armstrong for instance, he always, he could play with the worst band in the world and at times — not the worst band but guys that were just on a totally different — I mean nobody was on his level, but I mean like way under. And he would say that he didn’t even hear the band. Like he would just play, he just had such a strong feeling inside him that he would just charge ahead and it would just fall into place. And he could do that. And of course, that’s him. I cannot do that. I mean I can do that to a certain extent, but I always feel very much like I’m playing WITH the guys and that my playing is affected, for better or worse, by what’s going on around me. And sometimes I feel like it’s very difficult for me to like really get it happening if it doesn’t seem like it is around me. You know I’ll try, but I mean I guess that’s the nature of the thing. Sometimes you feel like it’s work, other times it’s just happening by itself. And it doesn’t necessarily, there are so many variables. It’s not just even the caliber of the musicians. The night after last we were doing this thing at Carnegie Hall and I played a few small group things. The rhythm section was terrific. Some of the tunes felt really good. The last tune we did, it just felt like — I mean I don’t know if the audience perceived this. But it just felt like for whatever reason it just was not coming together, it was an effort all the way through. And it sounded okay, I mean nobody did any obvious goofs. But to me it just didn’t really have the feeling. It wasn’t comfortable. And of course like when you don’t feel comfortable, there’s always a tendency to blame yourself. I think when you’ve got experience you tend to notice a little bit more that it’s other things too. But you just get into so many variables — the weather, how people are feeling, the acoustics.

event_label: Carnegie Hall Salutes the Jazz Masters: Verve Records at 50
speaker: JJ Johnson
caption: JJ Johnson recalls performing at the 1994 Jazz at the Philharmonic 50th anniversary celebration
text: Yes. In the next few weeks – as a matter of fact, the exact date is April 6, 1994 – there will be a celebration of 50 years of Jazz at the Philharmonic at Carnegie Hall – a black tie, high profile, prestigious event. They are even calling it the musical event of the year at Carnegie Hall in 1994. We’ll see about that. But a lot of people are excited about it. I will be involved. Illinois Jacquet, who was certainly a part of Jazz at the Philharmonic, will be there. A lot of people are on it that were not a part of Jazz at the Philharmonic, to make it a very high profile, prestigious event. I’m told that Shirley Horne is going to be . . . Carolyn, who are some of the other people who are going to be on the Carnegie Hall thing April the 6th? Shirley Horne and who else? CAROLYN JOHNSON: Abbey Lincoln.

event_label: Carnegie Hall Salutes the Jazz Masters: Verve Records at 50
speaker: JJ Johnson
caption: JJ Johnson recalls the April 6, 1994 Verve Records concert
text: Abbey Lincoln. A lot of people. A lot of people. I have the article here. I'll show it to you. It's interesting. It can explain in much more detail about what April the 6th at Carnegie Hall is all about than I can ever do. We saved it just for that purpose, 'cause I’ve tried a couple of times to explain to people and they say, "I don’t know what you're talking about." [Then I say,] "Here, read this." [And then they say,] "Oh! Now we know what you mean."

event_label: American Composers Orchestra
speaker: Jane Ira Bloom
caption: Premiere of Einstein’s Red/Blue Universe with ACO
text: Yeah. That was like a further incarnation of some of that NASA work. Basically that was a piece called “Einstein’s Red/Blue Universe,” and it was played by The American Composers Orchestra, a very fine new music orchestra in New York, conducted by Dennis Russell Davies, and it was my first piece for full symphony orchestra and jazz musicians, and sound surround brass choirs in the Carnegie Hall balconies. It was the funniest thing. We had — in order to maximize the use of the musicians there are points in the score where the brass section has to leave the stage and get up to the balcony in the Carnegie Hall. And that’s pretty precipitous up there by the way. They had to take the elevator to get up there in the middle of the piece.

event_label: Carnegie Hall Jazz Band: Sidney Bechet - A Centennial Celebration
venue: Isaac Stern Auditorium / Ronald O. Perelman Stage
speaker: Bob Wilber
caption: Tribute to Sidney Bechet with Jon Faddis
text: Yeah, it was. But you know what some of the jazz critics said? They said, “You’re tampering with a great record, you have no right to do that. I mean the second one of course was a Bechet masterpiece from 1944, four minutes of totally improvised blues, in which he tells his story, as he used to say when he was teaching me. He starts out with a very simple blues in the low register. Each chorus he builds, he goes up, he goes up and up and up. He finally reaches one of the highest notes on the clarinet, high E flat, a huge climax, and then he ends up down in the low register again. He ties the whole piece together thematically by the phrase that he uses on the last four bars of the twelve bar blues [scats]. He used this phrase every chorus. So it’s improvised composition, which is, to me, probably the highest form of jazz playing. It’s just not playing hot licks. It’s just not running up one chord and down another. It’s conceiving the whole performance as a single piece of music. One of the great classics. I had an interesting experience with this piece last year when I was asked by George Wein to play a concert at Carnegie Hall in a tribute to Bechet with Jon Faddis and the Carnegie Hall Big Band. So George’s idea with this band has always been to take elements of traditional music but combine them with elements of more contemporary you know, and make a synthesis. So I did that with this piece, and I started out, the whole piece a clarinet solo, I played all of Sidney’s notes, note for note. But as the piece developed I changed the rhythm of the band from 4/4 to 12/8. So gradually they’re going, instead of one-two-three-four — [scats fast tempo] And I started developing, as I played Sidney’s notes. And by the time I get at the last chorus the band is going [scats] and all these figures and everything. And he loved it. George said, “That’s great.” I mean it was a combination of the old and the new. So I’ve had a lot of fun with that piece. © Fillius Jazz Archive -18-

event_label: Carnegie Hall Jazz Band: Bernstein in Jazz
venue: Isaac Stern Auditorium / Ronald O. Perelman Stage
speaker: Dave Valentin
caption: Dave Valentin recalls playing West Side Story at Carnegie Hall
text: Yeah he played on some of the records, great, great, great, great man. In fact we just did that remake for the “West Side Story” also, where it was just piano and flute. And we did “I Feel Pretty” but with a whole new twist to it, which eventually had an Afro-Cuban thing but in seven-four. So it’s [scats]. And we did it at Carnegie Hall, we did that. And there’s a lull just before he starts his piano solo and he’s ready to go, and people start applauding. And they’re applauding, they’re applauding. So Dave gets up and says “the © Fillius Jazz Archive -11-

event_label: George Shearing's 80th: Celebrate with a Jazz Legend "Lullaby of Birdland"
venue: Isaac Stern Auditorium / Ronald O. Perelman Stage
speaker: Nancy Wilson
caption: Nancy Wilson recalls performing with George Shearing at his 80th birthday concert
text: But I loved George Shearing. He was precious. We did – when we worked at Carnegie Hall – I think it was – I don’t know who we were celebrating. Celebrating somebody’s birthday. I don’t think it was mine. Anyway, Shearing played the music that many years later exactly the way it was played on the record.

event_label: David Murray Quartet with Strings
speaker: David Murray
caption: David Murray's 2003 Carnegie Hall performance with strings
text: Well the near future is to mix this album. I’m playing Carnegie Hall on the fourteenth with my quartet with strings so I’m using ten — this time I’m using ten string players from New York. I hope they execute as well as the Cubans. I’m going to do this gig in Seattle I have to waste my time on doing. I probably will do a recording with Taj Mahal and Ishmael Reed, another Gwo-Ka with Kip Hanrahan. I think that’s on the books for October. November and December I’ll just probably be doing some gigs with the Gwo- Ka Masters. This little period of time right in here I’m concentrating on getting my kid in to a decent college. We’ll see, maybe he’ll wind up here.

event_label: Michael Kanan, Piano, and Jane Monheit, Singer
speaker: Jane Monheit
caption: Jane Monheit's duo recital with Michael Kanan
text: Yeah. Not while I was pregnant. I sang great when I was pregnant. Even when I was nine months pregnant. I did a gig at Carnegie Hall, at the recital hall at Carnegie, a duo gig with my pianist, maybe a week or ten days before my son was born, just out to here like waddling in flats and just singing, no problem. But the problem is you have this baby in you and it’s pressing on your diaphragm and then you have the baby and all the muscles are just kind of like flabby. And you know it takes a while to get your breathing back together and hormonal changes can affect your voices. And I have just recently been going through a period of vocal difficulty, you know my voice gets tired very, very easily. It’s been like seeing doctors and coaches and the whole thing, and every singer goes through this at some point and it’s very, very common to go through this sort of thing after having a child, especially now that my voice is being used more than ever. Not only are we on the road constantly, and jazz singers sing probably more than anyone else, because we’ll do like twelve shows a week. Everyone knows that I want to be on Broadway one day and people always say to me, “Oh, Jane, eight shows a week that’s a lot, can you handle it?” And I’m like, “Honey, I do twelve in six days all the time. And I fly with a 2-year-old with me.” You know, I’m like, “Please, I can handle it.” So we really sing a lot. And something that my brilliant voice teacher Joan Lader said to me recently that I never thought about it, she said for a singer like me a show is very stressful because I change genre constantly, and so it’s like several different kinds of singing. It’s not just like I’m doing one thing and staying in one place in terms of technique throughout the show, it’s constantly changing and she said that can be very stressful. And I never thought about it. So you know, I’m talking to my son all day, I’m singing to him all day, I’m doing two shows at night sometimes, there’s sound check or rehearsing, there’s always something going on. There’s traveling. There’s sleep deprivation. So it’s a lot, it’s a lot. Plus I speak improperly. I’m trying to learn to speak properly and I just can’t.

event_label: 3 Cohens Sextet: Anat, Avishai, and Yuval
speaker: Anat Cohen
caption: The Three Cohens Sextet's Carnegie Hall debut with mother in audience
text: they like something, they are into it and they are dedicated to it and they’re like okay we step out of the way, even if we have worries about how you’re going to survive and what’s going to happen, we’re going to let you do what you’re want to do and support you and help you pay for music lessons and drive you around and drive you around and drive you around, and go to all your youth orchestra concerts and, you know they were there when we were squeaking and they were there when The Three Cohens, with the three of us have a sextet and Avishai my younger brother the trumpet player, and Yuval, my older brother, the saxophone player, we have a sextet and when The Three Cohens Sextet played Carnegie Hall my mother was sitting in the audience and you know, and we introduced her and she got up and she was like doing the royal wave to everybody. So that’s the payment they get.

event_label: Anat Cohen Tentet
speaker: Anat Cohen
caption: Premiering Oded's 'Triple Helix' concerto with the Tentet
text: No the song “Putty Boy Strut” was composed by Flying Lotus, also known as Steve Allison. But Oded composed a concerto for the tentet and myself that we premiered in Carnegie Hall a couple of years ago and it’s the title album “Triple Helix” is the name of the concerto, it’s also the title album of the last tentet album that was nominated for a Grammy for best large ensemble. Exciting.
